data.title: Hims & Hers Health: At The Intersection Of Secular Megatrends
data.description:
Introduction

The share price of Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) has been extremely volatile since I recommended it as a Strong Buy in late 2024. Nevertheless, the stock delivered several massive bull runs within the last nine months and is currently 45% higher than it was when the initial recommendation was published. I am confident that this share price strength is explained by the company's stellar fundamentals and bright future prospects due to favorable secular trends. The forward P/E ratio trajectory looks extremely bullish, meaning that I continue buying with the reiterated Strong Buy recommendation.

Fundamental analysis

According to McKinsey's latest "Future of Wellness" survey, the wellness market is already an enormous $2 trillion industry. According to the study, the industry has great potential to grow further because Millennials and Gen Z are making wellness a daily, personalized priority, not just an occasional activity.

As the following picture suggests, younger generations are likely to spend more on wellness than older generations do, which is certainly a tailwind for the industry.

This survey from McKinsey is also very important while analyzing the potential of HIMS because it also reveals the most important wellness areas for younger generations. According to the survey, "sexual health and skin and hair care needs are more important to GenZers and millennials than to older generations." This trend is favorable for HIMS because the company excels in these areas.

What we know from common sense is that younger generations are also much more digitally savvy, which is a big tailwind for the digital health market. According to Grand View Research, the global digital health market is a thriving industry that is expected to observe a 22.2% CAGR by 2030.

With all that being said, it is obvious that HIMS is firmly positioned at this intersection of these secular megatrends driven by the changing preferences of younger demographic groups. The company is extremely successful in capitalizing on these tailwinds, which is apparent if we look at the TTM revenue curve. The latest reportable quarter saw a staggering 72.6% year-over-year revenue growth.

Another beautiful chart from HIMS is the company's TTM EBITDA growth. As we can see, the metric's growth repeats revenue, indicating solid potential to convert revenue growth into profits. Therefore, it is not surprising that Wall Street analysts expect HIMS to expand its EPS from $0.60 in FY2025 to $3.02 by FY2032, a massive five-fold increase.

Prudent capital allocation from the management is an important indication that they prioritize quality growth for shareholders. The company's outstanding share count has been relatively stable over the last several quarters, which is quite rare for an aggressively growing business. The total debt has increased from almost zero to one billion dollars during Q2, but it is still ten times lower than the company's market cap.

I also like that the management prioritizes investing in niches that are demonstrating aggressive growth and vast opportunities to capitalize on them. For example, HIMS demonstrates success in a very promising weight loss niche, which is particularly promising in the U.S., where obesity is a problem across even younger age groups. HIMS does not have enough scale to compete with leaders like Novo Nordisk or Eli Lilly in terms of R&D invested in GLP-1 drugs. However, it does not mean that HIMS does not have its competitive advantages. The company differentiates itself by providing a more personalized and holistic program for its customers with 24/7 team access, which pharmaceutical giants are highly likely to find too large to replicate.

Besides expanding into promising markets, HIMS also has endeavors in driving revenue growth by expanding internationally. The company made some bold moves in this respect during the summer of 2025. HIMS announced that it will start its expansion to Canada in 2026, once the company completes the acquisition of ZAVA, which is expected to help in its European expansion.

Let me finalize my analysis of fundamentals by adding a little bit of technical analysis here. The RSI indicator of HIMS has increased from 35.78 (almost oversold) to 51 within just two days, which might indicate that the share price trend is reversing because the performance of the HIMS share price over the last three months was a bit disappointing.

Valuation analysis

The forecasted forward P/E ratio trajectory of HIMS looks absolutely bullish because the multiple is expected to shrink massively between FY2025 and FY2028. The projected EPS growth looks realistic considering the ability of HIMS to capitalize on favorable secular trends.

Bullish long-term EPS growth outlook from Wall Street is backed by the projected aggressive revenue growth rate. The company's forward P/S ratio sits at 4.3 at the moment, which is just slightly above the sector median of 4. This gap might be unfairly narrow because HIMS demonstrates unparalleled growth across all key financial metrics compared to peers.

Wall Street analysts forecast FY2027 revenue to be $3.3 billion. Multiplying this value by the sector median P/S ratio of 4 means that the fair market cap by the end of FY2026 is around $13.2 billion. This means that HIMS has a 26% upside potential (compared to the current market cap of $10.5 billion) over the next 16 months, which looks compelling. Therefore, I think that HIMS is very attractive at the moment.

Mitigating factors

The healthcare sector is strictly governed, and any modifications in rules could impact not only the organization’s ongoing activities but also its expansion objectives. Failure to deliver on expansion initiatives could result in a slower-than-anticipated sales increase or reduced operational efficiency.

Any successful sector draws in additional new competitors, particularly those with minimal financial hurdles to entry. Relatively low investment needs are reflected in the company’s cash flow report.

The stock price increased by 229% over the last twelve months, which is highly likely to tempt some investors to bank profits. HIMS is an extremely volatile stock, with the share price range over the last month spanning between $13.5 and almost $73.

Conclusion

HIMS remains a compelling buying opportunity due to its rock-solid fundamentals and the bright future of the entire wellness industry. The stock still offers compelling upside over the next 16 months, which certainly outweighs potential risks and uncertainties.

data.title: YTD Asset Class Performance Through August 2025
data.description:
Until this year, US stocks had consistently been the best-performing asset class over the past 15 years, so diversification did not "work." But this year, diversification outside the US into foreign stocks has added value, and gold is shining very brightly.

Consequently, diversified target date funds are winning this year. Most TDFs are concentrated in US stocks and bonds.

Diversification

Here's a comparison of TDF glidepaths, highlighting the greater diversification in the SMART TDF Index versus the S&P TDF Index.

Now What

And so it's beginning. US stocks are becoming less attractive as they become even more expensive and other asset classes like gold and foreign stocks take the lead. Several articles have documented the fact that the US stock market is currently very expensive, so in a "bubble" that is likely to burst as it has in previous Minsky Moments. Your reaction to this bubble threat is driven by several factors that determine your risk tolerance, like your age, investment horizon, and your gut. It's much more than "Do I feel lucky today?"

Fear and greed are powerful motivators. Warren Buffet advises “be fearful when others are greedy and to be greedy only when others are fearful.” Bubbles keep expanding when others are greedy. Young people with a long horizon can be greedy because they can ride out the next correction, but older people have a much shorter horizon and less risk capacity.

As I've written previously in articles like "Revenge of the Baby Boomers" – that was read by more than 80,000 SA readers – older people near retirement should consider moving to safety while they are currently in the Retirement Risk Zone that spans the five years before and after retirement when they are exposed to Sequence of Return Risk.

How are you reacting? Is there a bubble?

Conclusion

The benefit of diversification is better risk-adjusted returns, but we usually see unadjusted returns, so diversification appears to have been unrewarded in the previous 15 years, but now we see the benefit this year even in unadjusted returns.

data.title: UMB Financial: Fundamentals Increasingly Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of UMB Financial (NASDAQ:UMBF) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 20%. With the company successfully beginning to integrate Heartland Financial after its acquisition, we are seeing strong financial results, pushing the stock towards a 52-week high. Since I last covered shares in May, UMB Financial has gained 23%, outpacing the market’s 15% rally and exceeding even my own expectations. With Fed rate cuts appearing imminent amidst a slowing labor market, now is a good time to see if UMBF has further upside.

UMB Financial is a mid-sized regional bank with about $72 billion of assets. It primarily operates in the central and Southwest portions of the United States with a network of nearly 200 branches. Earlier this year, UMB Financial completed its acquisition of Heartland Financial, greatly improving its scale. Still, the company remains solidly below the $100 billion asset threshold, providing it meaningful room for further growth before facing additional regulatory costs.

Thus far, the integration appears to be on track. In the company’s second-quarter earnings, reported on July 29, UMB Financial earned $2.82 per share, which beat estimates by $0.78. As you can see below, operating earnings more than doubled from last year to $225 million. Operating EPS was up a more modest 37%, given the increase in the share count for the Heartland purchase. Still, this deal is already proving to be substantially accretive, even with the higher share count.

Given the acquisition, year-over-year comparisons have limited utility, as essentially every metric is up substantially. However, sequential results speak to how well the company is performing even amidst integration. This picture continues to be encouraging. Deposits were up nearly 67% from last year to $60 billion. Again, I think looking at sequential trends is more useful. They grew by nearly $1.5 billion, or 10% annualized, a very strong pace. UMB has a very resilient deposit base; 46% of deposits have been with the bank over 10 years. A healthy 26% of deposits are noninterest-bearing (“NIB”), reducing funding costs. UMBF had a 3.44% cost of interest-bearing deposits, flat to last quarter, and I expect to see declines in H2 given Fed rate cuts.

On the other side of its balance sheet, loans were $36.8 billion, up 50% from a year ago. Sequentially, loans grew by about $870 million, or a 10% annualized pace. Heartland’s balance sheet was less loan-intensive than UMBF’s, and I expect UMB to work to grow loans more quickly than deposits to reduce excess liquidity and improve margins. On the earnings call, management said the headwinds initially felt in April amid tariff uncertainty have faded away. As policy uncertainty declines, we should see lending growth improve further, in my view.

In terms of loan quality, UMBF has a diversified loan book. Still, about 29% of the loan portfolio is in investment commercial real estate. This sector has faced more challenges, but UMB’s underwriting is strong. The average loan-to-value is just 57%, and 85% of the portfolio has recourse, both of which should greatly limit losses. Additionally, just 4% of the total portfolio is in office, and with more employers bringing workers back into the office, this exposure is less problematic than even a few months ago.

UMB has a history of strong underwriting, and that continues to be the case. As you can see below, its delinquencies are lower than a year ago at 8 bps. Bringing in Heartland, we have seen a jump in delinquencies, but even here, there was a very encouraging improvement sequentially. As we see UMB apply its more stringent underwriting standards over the next year, I expect consolidated delinquencies to improve. Meanwhile, UMB is carrying $390 million of allowances, up by $21 million. This provides about 4x coverage of its $97 million in nonperforming loans (NPLs), well above my 2.5x threshold, meaning credit performance could get worse before UMB really needs to add more to reserves.

UMB also has a $17 billion securities portfolio, largely of fixed-income securities bought when interest rates were lower. As such, it has a ~$450 million unrealized loss on the portfolio. As bonds mature and get either reinvested in new bonds or used to fund loan growth, we will see the portfolio yield continue to improve. Indeed, there were $605 million of cash flows in Q2 at 3.11%; UMB reinvested at 4.63%. About $1.8 billion of securities mature over the next year. The securities portfolio has about a 5-year duration, so this will be a prolonged but steady build in interest income.

Because of this, UMB’s net interest margin [NIM] was 3.01%, but this was aided by purchase accounting following its acquisition of Heartland. Excluding this, NIM was 2.83%, up 8 bps sequentially. Net interest margin was 2.51% last year. I expect a modest further improvement in Q3. I would note that net interest income was boosted by $42 million in Q2 thanks to the accretion of loans and securities held by Heartland. Another $714 million remains to be recognized, which will occur over the next few years. We see at least $180 million of this being realized by the end of next year. To the extent that loans are paid off early, we could see more of an impact.

One other item to consider about the forward outlook is that the company did have a $29 million gain from its stake in Voyager Technologies (VOYG), which IPOed in the quarter. It has ~904,000 shares. Given where shares are trading today, we will see a ~$12 million loss as shares have pulled back from their post-IPO highs, meaning Q3 will likely be sequentially weaker than Q2. I would expect UMBF to exit this investment by the end of next year, though it has no commitment to do so. UMB has several investments via its private investment team, and with valuations rising and the IPO market improving, there may be other potential gains.

Importantly, UMB remains a well-capitalized bank. It has a 10.4% common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio, which is 9.5% CET1 adjusting for AOCI. Given it has less than $100 billion in assets, it does not need to include AOCI losses in its calculation, but this is still relevant when considering its economic capital position. I expect to see capital continue to build and potentially reach 10% inclusive of AOCI within 12 months, which could then enable buybacks alongside its secure 1.3% dividend yield.

Now, my expectation is for the Federal Reserve to cut rates two times this year and at least two times next year. However, if the economic outlook weakens further, we could see a faster pace of rate cuts. Because most of its loan book is floating rate (as is the case for most regional banks), rate cuts are a headwind to net interest income. As you can see below, rate cuts reduce UMB’s potential net interest income, with a larger impact over time, as it takes some time for loans to reset (some loans reset monthly, others quarterly, etc.). If we see the Fed cut rates more aggressively, that does pose a downside risk to earnings, though it would also reduce the bank’s AOCI loss.

Back in May, I saw UMBF having about $10.50 in 2025 EPS with the potential to grow to $11 next year as it has a full year of Heartland contributing. I still see UMBF earning $10.50, +/- $0.10 this year. Given my assumption of 4-5 rate cuts over the next year, I still see the bank positioned for at least $11.00 of EPS next year. In this market environment, I view ~9-12x as the fair multiple for regional banks, depending on their growth and capital positioning. Given tailwinds from growing its loan book and its solid loan quality, I believe UMBF should trade in the upper tier or about 11x forward earnings.

This suggests ~$121 is fair value for shares, which is near where shares are trading today. Particularly with the potential for more rate cuts, I see less upside in shares of UMBF, and I see momentum slowing in the stock going forward. Still, given its solid fundamentals and trading close to fair value, I do not see a need to sell shares. I am downgrading shares to a “Hold” and see them as performing in line with the market. If there was a pullback below $115, I would consider buying, but for now there is no rush to add shares.

data.title: OneConnect: The New Business Model
data.description:
OneConnect Financial Technology (NYSE:OCFT) stock is a hold, as it has a very high risk in its restructuring execution, with high cash burn and a lack of profitability while still operating at a loss. The next quarters will be essential for the company's fate, and if the stabilization of its margins will happen, with its indicators becoming positive.

The Company

Is a Technology as a Service (TaaS) provider, operating mostly in China for financial institutions with a growing ambition for global expansion. The company’s main shareholder, Ping An Group (OTCPK:PNGAY), is also one of its largest clients, and this association offers positive and negative points for OCFT, in which on one hand it gains credibility in the market, however, on the other, it creates dependence on PNGAY, becoming evident when the revenue from Ping An and Lufax fell by 59%, directly affecting the company's total revenue.

OneConnect went through a restructuring where it eliminated 99.6% of its cloud platform revenue as an extreme change, representing an implicit admission that the previous business model was unsustainable, as its very high operating expenses led to low profit margins. After this restructuring, it focused on 3 main segments:

  • Digital Banking, which offers solutions for retail banks, digital credit management, and core banking systems, and despite being the main focus of its new business, it showed a decline in the first half of this year due to macroeconomic issues, since its compensation is made by transactions, and with the slowdown in the economy, banks reduced credit granting.

  • Digital Insurance, a segment for solutions for the auto, health, and life insurance sectors, becoming a positive point for the company, growing by 11.2%, indicating that among the three, it is the most promising, being the only one that did not show a decline.

  • Gamma Platform, a platform that offers a basis for solutions, being the epicenter of the restructuring, and despite showing a decline like Digital Banking, it was a planned and direct decline to focus on other more profitable areas.

H1 25 Results

At the end of August, the company reported its results for the first half of this year under its new model. Its revenue from continuing operations was approximately $112 million, a 43.4% decrease compared with last year, and this drop was primarily driven by the deliberate phasing out of its low-margin cloud services segment whose revenue fell 99.6%. The company's gross margin compressed to 26.1% from 37.1% in the same period last year, reflecting the loss of scale. The Digital Insurance segment grew 11.2%, demonstrating strength in the company's new strategic focus area.

Sector

OneConnect operates in the Fintech as a Service ((FaaS)) market, which at the moment, is undergoing rapid expansion and growth. On a global scale, this market was valued at $417 billion in the year 2025, with growth rates between 14% to 16.3% until 2035, being expected to pass $1 trillion. In the Asia-Pacific, China is the main driver of this market which in 2024 was valued at $4.59 trillion, with projections for its value to double by 2030, reaching almost $10 trillion.

The trends of this market in the country mostly involve the digitalization of the sector, as its economy is considered cashless, being directly driven by the presence of payment apps that create a base for active consumers in the digital environment, generating a demand for digital financial services, and with the integration of AI and big data being applied for credit enhancement and fraud detection, improving the efficiency for these consumers.

OCFT is well-positioned to capture these trends, with its insurtech segment being of higher growth in the latest results, being able to double this number if it focuses on this trend, along with its AI and big data tools, which have easy integration and can thus be contracted by small to medium-sized banks, to specifically obtain the fraud detection tool, reducing the implementation cost.

Competition

This market is large, therefore, the competition within it is fierce, having large companies that are already strong and established. The leaders are financial and digital companies, such as Alibaba and Tencent, which have millions of users on their platforms, which are used daily by these consumers, therefore, the company's strategy of selling its technologies to banks and insurers is an attempt to position itself as a facilitator, with its success depending entirely on the willingness of these institutions to invest in competitive technologies against the already consolidated companies.

The company's expansion into the international market comes not only from a growth opportunity but from a need to diversify its geographic and regulatory risk from China, where the government increases regulatory issues and the local economy slows, leaving companies with an exclusively Chinese market more vulnerable and dependent.

Valuation

Revenue

The company generates revenues through the B2B model, offering services to companies in the financial niche, such as banks, insurers, asset managers, etc. Its revenue streams come from fees, separated into Implementation, generated from the construction and installation of its technology, and Transaction, from processed transactions, monthly subscriptions, and technical or maintenance support, these being recurring revenue services.

The projection for Transaction Revenue is negatively impacted by the failure in the expansion strategy and the continuous reduction in spending by client companies, and with that, a decline will be projected for its segments, even with the cloud segment reaching zero, because the portfolio cleanup in this scenario was not offset by new business, thus resulting in a lower revenue than in the previous period, showing a loss of -29.3%, reaching $153.4 million. The projection for Implementation Revenue is made from a waterfall format table, where the result of one quarter is used as the basis for the next, in which each period a minimal and insufficient revenue of $1.8 million from new contractors is added, along with the loss of -$0.5 regarding the conclusion of old projects, this being a continuous negative impact, also leaving the revenue lower than in the year 2024, reaching only $83.3 million.

Starting from 2022 the scenario changed, and revenues began to show continuous drops motivated by the macroeconomic and regulatory scenario in China, which became increasingly difficult, thus directly impacting OneConnect's clients. With this economy growing at a slow pace, there was lower demand in credit granting and other financial activities, in addition to the reduction of its clients' budgets, who due to pressure on its profitability, had to cut or postpone projects that involved investments in technologies, thus resulting in less revenue.

For the projection of the last quarters of 2025, the scenario continues to show a drop in revenue, assuming that the company's recovery strategy did not work as expected, considering that it operates at a loss and do not generate profits. In Q3 the implementation revenue falls to $19.9 million, reflecting the difficulty in closing new international contracts and a greater termination of projects in China, with the transaction revenue also showing a drop to $39.1 million, motivated by the reduction in client spending, and the sum of the segments totals a revenue of $59.0 million, a drop of -2.48% compared to Q2. For Q4, the seasonal improvement will not happen, considering that the implementation revenue will continue to show drops, falling to $19.5 million, with the transaction client base also decreasing, leading the segment's revenue to $38.7 million, resulting in a total revenue of $58.2 million.

For 2026, the pessimistic projection shows an acceleration in this decline, with the failure of international expansion becoming evident, where the first quarter with the lack of new international contracts will lead the implementation revenue to fall to $19 million, accompanied by the transaction segment which will decrease to $38.1 million, due to continuous pressure on clients, with the total result showing a drop of -1.89%. In Q2 the downward trend increases, thus causing the implementation to reach $18.6 million, and the transaction revenue to continue decreasing to $37.3 million, resulting in a drop of -2.1%.

EBITDA

Its EBITDA is currently at -$23.13, this being a clear sign of OCFT's inefficiency in generating profits, indicating that the profit from its services is not enough to cover the most basic operating costs and expenses, even before considering debt interest, depreciation, or taxes. In the long run, a negative EBITDA places great pressure on the company's management, so that its new business model not only generates revenue but is also profitable.

Historically, the EBITDA has always been negative, a characteristic present since it became public. The company always focused on accelerated growth, investing heavily in R&D, sales, and marketing, and this approach kept it at an operating loss for many years, which forced it to cut costs, and eventually, start its restructuring since the growth at any cost was not sustainable.

EV/EBITDA

Because its EV/EBITDA is negative at -3.26x, the multiple to be used to calculate the stock's price target in 2026 is its P/S, currently at 1.16x. This number is a reflection of the pessimism and the uncertainties surrounding the company's, considering that the other companies in the FaaS sector trade this multiple between 5x to 10x, which make its current value is extremely low. Several factors can justify this value, such as the reflection of its revenue collapse in recent years, and with projections pointing to declines, the market has priced it in advance in relation to this. The absence of profitability is also a relevant factor, because in addition to operating at a loss, it also burns cash, leaving its P/S as one of the few applicable metrics.

Using this metric and the revenue projection for the next 12 months of $230.2 million, its stock and its Market Cap will show a decrease of 5.86%, with the price per share starting from $7.21 currently, reaching $6.78, a decline value already presented in previous moments, and the Market Cap starting from the current value of $283.62 million, decreasing to $267 million.

Net Income

Operating at a net loss is also a characteristic of the company, demonstrating a significant net loss with the difficult periods it has been facing. Currently, this number is -$94.5, larger than the previous year, not being the reflection of just one bad quarter, but an accumulation of all its problems, with low margins and the collapse in revenue. Despite this, the losses in the first quarter of 2025 were significantly smaller than the second half of 2024. Because it is consistently negative, the path to the company's profitability depends entirely on its operating leverage, in which after many cost cuts, its expense base is smaller, thus needing only consistent revenues that will bring better gross margins.

Drivers and Risks

In my point of view, the most relevant growth driver for OCFT is its expansion into the international scope, this being the pillar of its current business. The company has already guaranteed its presence in places like Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Africa, counting on providing services to 214 financial institutions, in 20 different countries, with some concrete contracts such as Old Mutual in South Africa, and CIMB in the Philippines. The growth of revenues from clients outside of China would be the sign that this model is working, being able to increase not only the company's revenues but also its profitability, which would consequently make its margins and multiples rise.

The most prominent risk at this moment for OneConnect is its dependence on the Ping An Group, because as previously mentioned, the drop in its revenue and from Lufax is the most imminent risk. Any other deterioration in this relation that is fundamental for the company will have an impact on its revenue, leaving it vulnerable to contract renegotiations as it has low negotiating power. This strong association with Ping An can also make sales to the group's direct competitors difficult, who may see the company as an arm of a rival rather than as an independent supplier company.

Although its net cash position is a long-term mitigator, it is not infinite, therefore, the company operating at a loss and with operational cash burn is a financial and operational risk. With the continuation of this loss the reserve will continue to be consumed, raising the question of whether the company will be able to reach a break-even point before this cash runs out.

Conclusion

The company at the moment has high risks, such as the absence of profitability, operating at a loss and burning a lot of cash, and its lack of proof regarding its recovery, and it cannot be considered a sell option because it still has financial security, such as the fact that its cash is greater than its debt, for example. At the moment, the best is to hold the stock, and wait for more evidence of a real improvement from the company.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Kraken Robotics, VICI Properties, AeroVironment And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today we're looking at articles published between Aug. 29 and Sep. 4.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

QDTE Is Another High-Yield Champion, At Least For The Past Year

Damon Judd | Buy

As we begin to approach what appears to be another potential top in the market in September 2025, there remains plenty of interest in high yield income vehicles from retail investors. While I hold many income investments in my own Income Compounder portfolio (between 30 and 40 holdings), it is the higher yielding, riskier ones that seem to capture the most interest from readers on this site. Case in point is my recent review of YMAX vs. YMAG, which has gotten above-average page views and more than 90 comments as of today, 9/3/25. Conversely, my review of the safer, lower-yielding review of the DNP Select Income Fund (DNP) has received less than half the page views and has about 30 comments so far.

This time around I want to take a look at the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:QDTE), and compare results with other similar high-yield income funds that write covered calls against tech stocks. One thing that is unique about QDTE compared to some of its peers is that the fund pays weekly distributions, while most of its peers pay out monthly (except YMAG, which also pays weekly).

VICI Properties: I'm Backing Up The Truck Despite Las Vegas Tourism Slump

Pacifica Yield | Buy

VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) has built a one-of-a-kind portfolio of high-quality gaming and entertainment destination assets, subject to long-term triple-net leases that provide for annual base rent escalations. This has set the backdrop for sustained adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth and a healthy dividend yield. I'm bullish but flagged risks from the Las Vegas tourism slump when I last covered the ticker. VICI last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.4325 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.73 per share annualized for a 5.18% dividend yield. The REIT last raised its dividend by 4% in September 2024 and has compounded its distributions by 7.77% annually since 2020. This is set to continue, with VICI being able to lean on multiple levers for growth.


AeroVironment Earnings Preview: Ready For A Defense Supercycle Rerating

Agar Capital | Strong Buy

I am bullish on AeroVironment, Inc. (NASDAQ:AVAV) with a target price of $300 (+25% upside from now). The company is at the heart of the new defense supercycle, producing drones, loitering munitions, and anti-drone systems. With the acquisition of BlueHalo, its TAM has multiplied by 5, opening up a huge market. Government interest and military spending are on the rise, and in the following paragraphs, I will explain why I believe this positioning justifies a higher multiple than in the past.


How To Profit From Underwater Warfare? - Initiating Kraken Robotics With A Buy

Tech Stock Pros | Buy

Kraken Robotics Inc. (OTCQB:KRKNF) is an under-the-radar and underwater small-cap Canadian company that deserves more attention from the retail investor because attention now will be well-rewarded later, in our opinion.

The company is a leader within the synthetic aperture sonar (SAS) market; they work on underwater imaging tech as well as subsea batteries to transform subsea intelligence and how we use it. The company just announced $13B in orders for its SAS and subsea batteries from customers in the U.S.. Norway and Turkey, one of which includes 10 SAS orders for one customer. The stock closed almost 5% higher on the news on Wednesday, bringing its six-month rally to 72%.


Northwest Natural Has Value, Growth, And Yield

Gen Alpha | Buy

Successful investing doesn’t have to be a popularity contest. In fact, some of my best picks are ones off the beaten path and can be found in both bull and bear markets alike. That’s because it’s a market for stocks rather than the stock market, and there is always something on sale due to market irrationality. Sooner or later, the market pendulum will swing back toward quality dividend stocks that are undervalued, and patient investors will reap the reward while getting paid a solid income stream in the meantime.

This brings me to Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN), which I last covered in June, noting its mix of value and income and strategic expansion. What’s great for value investors is that NWN has been rangebound, and currently trades just 0.44% higher than when I last covered it, offering opportunities to layer into the stock. As shown below, NWN currently trades in the middle of its 52-week range while sporting a 4.7% yield.


Tempus AI: Expansive Opportunity As Healthcare Becomes More Heavily Scrutinized

Michael Del Monte | Buy

Tempus AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:TEM) is navigating a market that may provide multiple positive catalysts as the healthcare and pharmaceutical industries become more heavily scrutinized, potentially driving the need for more data-driven services. With the recently announced tariffs on pharmaceuticals, a substantial amount of investments in manufacturing and R&D have been announced destined for the U.S., bolstering Tempus’s addressable market stateside. Valuing TEM shares, I believe shares should trade at $84/share at 9.52x eFY26 price/sales; I am rating TEM shares with a Buy.


Seagate Technology: Ahead In Technology, Behind In Growth

Jia Ming Eow | Hold

Seagate Technology Holdings plc (NASDAQ:STX) has soared nearly 3x since the April lows, with an increase in demand for high-capacity storage for AI workloads as well as heat-assisted magnetic recording (HAMR) storage, which is advancing fast. With cyclicality favouring Seagate, it is running off huge momentum, but the trend needs to show that it can break past its all-time high revenues back in 2022, as the storage market seems to be rebounding since 2024. Margins are expanding compared to years prior, but the high debt, relative valuation, and high relative strength index suggest that the potential upside and growth trajectory for future years are unclear with high cyclicality. Hence, I'm rating Seagate at a 'Hold'.


Bloom Energy: Options And Dark Pools Signal Steady Momentum, But Valuation Screams Sell

Everyday Options | Sell

Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) just posted Q2 earnings that looked, at first glance, exactly how bulls would want it. Revenue is up nearly 20%, margins are ticking higher, and cash flow is finally stable. You can see why investors are tempted to stay long here. And long they have stayed, as Bloom is up over 340% in just one year.

But when I dig through the numbers and stack them against valuation, the setup feels very different. Bloom is trading like it can execute perfectly from here on out, with multiples that leave almost no room for error. That doesn't make the operating progress any less real, but it does mean the price is already sprinting ahead of fundamentals. And in this kind of environment, I think it's fair to ask if the risk-reward balance has tilted the wrong way.


Cal-Maine Foods: Cash Flow Consistency And Dividends

Rogerio Adelino, CFA | Hold

Cal-Maine Foods (NASDAQ:CALM) is the largest producer and supplier of eggs in the US. Despite being in a less trendy industry, the stock price performed very well in a year-to-date comparison with the S&P 500, besides counting on a strong dividend yield, low beta, and a Strong Buy Quant Rating. The firm’s financial performance is, on the other hand, very influenced by external factors that may directly regulate the volatile price of the egg. On the bright side, the firm shows consistent financial discipline to benefit from higher prices for the benefit of stockholders, magnifying its return.


Nextracker: Valuation And The End Of Political Drama Suggest Taking Profits

Vince Martin | Hold

Fundamentally, Nextracker (NASDAQ:NXT) stock looks ridiculously cheap. Even with a 65% rally year-to-date, shares trade at a little over 16x guidance for fiscal 2026 (ending March) adjusted earnings per share. Given clear leadership in solar trackers, impressive growth (adjusted EPS rose 38% last year, though there's a bit of a politically driven pause on that front this year), and solar's long-term outlook, that multiple looks almost impossibly low.

That's because the multiple is somewhat deflated: The Section 45X tax credit is boosting profit margins currently, though that benefit will start to fade in a few years. Adjusted for 45X, the multiples assigned to Nextracker stock look much more reasonable – and perhaps high enough to incorporate even impressive growth through the end of the decade. Given relief around the industry and the impact of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" signed in July, there is a case here that NXT at this point is reasonably priced.

Indeed, the consensus sell-side estimate now projects just 7% appreciation over the next 12 months. And it's not terribly difficult to see why. This is an excellent business – one perhaps worth owning longer-term – but valuation simply is much more aggressive than a first glance suggests. At this point, there do seem to be better options elsewhere in the space, and at the very least, real questions about near-term catalysts for Nextracker stock.


Lockheed Martin: Valuation Isn't All That Matters

Daniel Jones | Hold

To be honest with you, I don't really write about the war economy all that much. But every so often, I'd like to dip my toes into this space. When it comes to Lockheed Martin Corporation (NYSE:LMT), I must admit that it has been quite a while since I've written anything mentioning it. The last time I tagged it in an article was back in 2017. Looking at the fundamental picture today, I see a business that has had some mixed results in recent years. Revenue is rising at a respectable rate. And with U.S. military spending certain to increase moving forward, that trend should continue. For this year, financial expectations look mixed. Given how the company is priced compared to other similar firms, I would normally consider rating it a "Buy". But in light of some risks and recent underperformance from a profit and cash flow perspective, I believe rating it a "Hold" is more appropriate.


ASA: Gold Miners Are Just Getting Warmed Up

Power Hedge | Buy

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited (NYSE:ASA) is a Bermudan closed-end fund, or CEF, that specializes in investing in gold and other precious metals miners. Despite generally getting little attention in the financial media, precious metals mining companies have been among the best-performing firms in the global markets year-to-date. We can see this in the fact that the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX), which tracks the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index, has gained 92.27% since January 1, 2025, easily trouncing the S&P 500 Index (SP500).

For its part, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has been no slouch, as its share price is up 92.04% since the start of the year, comparable to the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index and even outperforming it at certain times.


Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VHT: A Popular Choice For Investing In U.S. Health Care Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

The Vanguard Health Care Index ETF (NYSEARCA:VHT) provides passive exposure to almost 400 U.S. companies operating in the Health Care sector, as determined by Global Industry Classification Standards, or GICS. With a 0.09% expense ratio and $17.60 billion in assets under management, VHT is the go-to choice for many shareholders looking to efficiently reduce their risk in a market increasingly dominated by mega-cap growth stocks. In this article, I will explain VHT's strategy, highlight how it tends to perform in bull and bear markets, and discuss from a factor perspective how VHT compares with other U.S. Health Care ETFs you might be considering. I hope you enjoy the read.

Vanguard Health Care Index ETF Overview

Strategy Discussion and Composition

According to the Vanguard website, VHT tracks the MSCI US Investable Market Health Care 25/50 Index, which is comprised of companies in the large, mid, and small-cap segments operating in the following ten sub-industries:

The Index is free-float market-cap-weighted, though the Index caps each security's weight at 25% at each quarterly rebalancing, ensuring compliance with the U.S. Internal Revenue Code. The Index also limits the combined allocation of all securities with weights above 5% to 50%, and after applying a 10% buffer, the effective caps are 22.5% and 45%, respectively. However, these caps don't really apply to U.S. Health Care stocks. As shown below, Eli Lilly (LLY) is VHT's top holding, but it only had a 10.80% allocation as of July 31, 2025 (10.24% currently). AbbVie (ABBV) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) also have allocations above 5%, but combined, the total of these three is just 21%, so neither cap is close to taking effect.

VHT Fund Facts

I've spliced together several fund statistics from the Vanguard website below, including VHT's $17.60 billion in assets under management, 0.04% 30-day median bid-ask spread, and 289K 50-day average volume, metrics which indicate VHT is well-established and highly liquid. The fund is comprised of 406 holdings (including cash), had a minimal 4.10% turnover rate for the most recent fiscal year, and also had a 1.63% trailing dividend yield as of July 31, 2025, suggesting income is not a primary investment objective.

In addition, VHT's expense ratio is only 0.09%, making it the third-cheapest U.S. Health Care sector ETF behind the Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) and the Fidelity MSCI Health Care ETF (FHLC), both of which charge 0.08% and whose fundamentals I will analyze alongside VHT shortly. Other popular options in the Health Care space include the iShares U.S. Health Care ETF (IYH) and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Health Care ETF (RSPH), though their fees are much higher at 0.38% and 0.40%, respectively.

Vanguard Health Care Index ETF Analysis

VHT Performance vs. The S&P 500 Index

I'd like to start this performance analysis by comparing VHT's total returns against the broader market, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). From February 1, 2004, until September 3, 2025, VHT delivered an annualized 9.30% compared to 10.37% for SPY, which has added up to a rather large 162% cumulative total return difference (748% vs. 586%). Still, VHT offered important protection over the last two decades, evidenced by competitive downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio), a maximum drawdown that was 16% better, and a "worst year" figure that was 13% better. At 0.78, VHT's correlation to SPY is low for an equity ETF, and these results suggest it can be a good choice for investors looking to reduce their risk.

Digging deeper, I noticed that VHT actually outperformed SPY several times throughout its history. For example, the following rolling returns chart shows VHT easily outperforming SPY for the three-year periods ending February 2009 and May 2015. In contrast, VHT lagged SPY by 5.77% per year from December 2018 to December 2021, and as of August 31, 2025, VHT's 4.79% annualized three-year return sits almost 15% behind SPY, the largest gap to date. For investors believing in "mean reversion", this may represent a compelling "buy" opportunity.

VHT Performance vs. Other Vanguard Sector ETFs

Since October 2004, VHT has gained 9.91% per year, making it the fourth-best performer in Vanguard's suite of sector ETFs behind the Vanguard Information Technology ETF (VGT), the Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR), and the Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS). VHT was also the second-least volatile ETF, as measured by its 13.98% annualized standard deviation. Together, this made VHT's risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino Ratios) third-best behind VGT and the Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC).

Notably, VHT ranked average or better on all seven metrics listed above. As shown below, it also ranked #2/11 on the "worst year" and "maximum drawdown" figures, and its 42.67% "best year" result ranked #6/11. For long-term investors, these results are encouraging, so I see it as a possible buy-and-hold ETF that can reliably reduce risk. The same goes for VDC and the Vanguard Utilities ETF (VPU), though VPU doesn't rank as highly on risk-adjusted returns.

VHT Fundamental Analysis vs. Other Health Care ETFs

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for VHT's top 25 holdings, which comprise 72.74% of the portfolio as of September 4, 2025. Notably, this concentration level is almost identical to FHLC, and since they overlap by 99.49%, they are essentially the same fund, although some readers might have a preference for Vanguard or Fidelity ETFs.

Here are two takeaways to consider:

Health Care: A Low Beta Sector

As we've seen in the performance charts, the U.S. Health Care sector is generally defensive, and in addition to its low standard deviation (total risk), the table above highlights the sector's low market risk (beta). At 0.67, VHT's five-year portfolio beta is nearly identical to XLV, IYH, and FHLC, while RSPH's 0.82 figure is quite a bit higher. These numbers indicate the defense comes from allocating more to large-cap Health Care stocks, something RSPH does not do with its equal-weight approach.

When compared to other Vanguard sector ETFs, VHT's five-year beta is the third-lowest behind the 0.62 figures for VPU and VDC (Utilities and Consumer Staples). This reinforces the concept that investing in a U.S. Health Care ETF is a simple way to reduce portfolio risk, and if you plan to hold for the long run, history suggests you'll also earn solid returns.

Large-Cap Health Care Stocks Have Better Quality, GARP Metrics

VHT, XLV, IYH, and FHLC have better net and free cash flow margins compared to RSPH, and even though they trade at slightly higher multiples, they offer better growth. To illustrate, we can calculate a modified PEG ratio for each ETF by dividing the one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate into the forward P/E ratio, as follows:

  • VHT: 17.56x / 14.04% = 1.25x
  • XLV: 17.33x / 13.57% = 1.28x
  • IYH: 17.26x / 14.41% = 1.20x
  • FHLC: 17.10x / 14.04% = 1.22x
  • RSPH: 14.79x / 8.50% = 1.74x

The larger ETFs also have better three-year historical sales and earnings per share growth rates, and along with their better margins, it's easy to argue they deserve their higher multiples. This helps to explain why RSPH has underperformed over the last five years, with edge going to XLV, the ETF with the largest weighted average market cap.

Summary and Conclusion

This article was written primarily for educational purposes and, as such, did not include a rating. Instead, my main objective was to answer questions like:

  1. What type of investor is VHT designed for?
  2. Is VHT most appropriate as a short- or long-term holding?
  3. How has VHT historically performed from a risk and return perspective?
  4. What are some alternatives to VHT in the Health Care category?

VHT currently offers investors double-digit earnings per share growth, and since its net and free cash flow margins are also above 10%, VHT could be considered a "defensive quality growth" ETF of sorts. Furthermore, the fund's negative three-year returns gap with SPY is the highest in its history, and factors like an aging population and investments in new technologies could lead to sustainable growth. National health care expenditures as a percentage of GDP are expected to accelerate from 17.6% to 20.3% over the next decade, compared to only a 0.7% increase from the prior decade, per the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

VHT has also consistently delivered strong returns in market downturns over the last 20 years. Specifically, its risk-adjusted returns, as measured by the Sharpe and Sortino Ratios, made it the third-best sector to invest in behind Technology and Consumer Staples, and on key performance metrics like total returns, standard deviation, and maximum drawdowns, VHT ranked above average or better compared to other Vanguard sector ETFs since its inception in 2004. Therefore, I suggest considering VHT as a long-term investment, depending on whether Health Care is underrepresented in your portfolio, but short-term uses also work to efficiently reduce risk.

I hope I have answered the above questions, but if anything is unclear or you are interested in learning more about the alternatives to VHT, please let me know in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: uniQure: Getting Nervous Ahead Of Critical Huntington's Gene Therapy Data
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered uniQure (NASDAQ:QURE) in a note for Seeking Alpha published in February, assigning stock a "Buy" rating (and acquiring some stock myself). Just over 6 months later, shares have risen in value by >35%.

uniQure is a gene therapy specialist biotech, headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands, valued at a market cap of $1.2B at the time of writing, with shares valued at $18 per share.

The company has secured a couple of product approvals in the past - Glybera (alipogene tiparvovec) was approved in Europe 2012 to treat the ultra-rare disease hereditary lipoprotein lipase deficiency (LPLD), but was withdrawn from the market in 2017, due to lack of sales, and Hemgenix (etranacogene dezaparvovec), approved in the US in 2022, and in Europe in 2023, to treat hemophilia B. CSL Behrig purchased the rights to market sell Hemgenix back in 2020 in a deal involving an upfront payment of $450m, up to $1.6bn of milestone payments, and "double digit royalties ranging up to a low-twenties percentage of net sales".

UniQure's present focus is on its lead clinical candidate, AMT-130, which it describes as:

our novel gene therapy candidate for the treatment of Huntington’s disease, which utilizes our proprietary, gene-silencing miQURE platform and incorporates an AAV vector carrying a micro ribonucleic acid (“miRNA”) specifically designed to silence the huntingtin gene and the potentially highly toxic exon 1 protein fragment

UniQure also provides an overview of the therapy in a recent investor presentation, shown below:

AMT-130 has been granted a Breakthrough Therapy and a Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy (“RMAT”), Orphan Drug and Fast Track designations by the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA"), and as I wrote in my last note:

It was AMT-130 news that sent uniQure soaring in December last year, from ~$6 per share, to ~$18 per share - a gain of ~200%. There was no data to share. However, management announced that it had "reached agreement with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) on key elements of an Accelerated Approval pathway for AMT-130".

An accelerated approval is an FDA program designed to:

allow for earlier approval of drugs that treat serious conditions, and fill an unmet medical need based on a surrogate endpoint", i.e. a marker, such as a laboratory measurement, radiographic image, physical sign or other measure that is thought to predict clinical benefit but is not itself a measure of clinical benefit,

A drug granted accelerated approval is expected to then undergo a post-confirmatory study that must demonstrate a clinical benefit, and risk being withdrawn from the market if that study fails, although such studies can last years.

In clinical studies, uniQure has shown that AMT-130 can achieve a statistically significant and dose-dependent slowing of disease progression, and a statistically significant reduction in cerebrospinal fluid ("CSF") (see slide below), with a "manageable" safety profile.

In its 2024 annual report / 10K filing uniQure defines Huntington's Disease as follows:

Huntington’s disease is a severe genetic neurodegenerative disorder causing loss of muscle coordination, behavioral abnormalities, and cognitive decline, often resulting in complete physical and mental deterioration over a 12 to 15-year period. The median survival time after onset is 15 to 18 years (range: 5 to >25 years).

Huntington’s disease is caused by an inherited defect in a single gene that codes for a protein called Huntingtin (“HTT”). The estimated prevalence of Huntington’s disease is three to seven per 100,000 in the general population, similar in men and women, and it is therefore considered a rare disease.

Huntington’s disease mutation carriers can be identified decades before onset. There is currently no available therapy that can delay onset or slow progression of the disease. Although some symptomatic treatments are available, they only are transiently effective despite significant side effects.

In summary, then, my bullish take on uniQure was based on the likely approval of AMT-130, and its potential to elevate the standard of care in Huntington's disease.

Currently approved therapies Austedo, marketed and sold by Japanese Pharma Takeda (TAK), and Neurocrine Biosciences' (NBIX) Ingrezza - which earned respectively ~$1.7bn and $2.3bn last year - are designed to treat movement disorders as opposed to the disease itself, while Danish Pharma Lundbeck's Xenazine is another approved HD therapeutic.

Several companies - PTC Therapeutics (PTCT), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), Wave Life Sciences (WVE), Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY), and Regeneron (REGN), have therapies in development, but in my last note I suggested that AMT-130 would likely be first to market, potentially with a best-in-class efficacy and safety profile, which would likely lead to a "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenue opportunity for uniQure.

Since my note, uniQure stock has plunged, risen and plunged again in value, but as mentioned, is ultimately up >35% since my note, at the time of writing.

In this note, I'll recap on some of the developments that have contributed to that volatility, and discuss whether I remain as bullish in uniQure and AMT-130 as I was back in February. Let's begin with an overview of Q2 earnings, and management's earnings call.

uniQure Post Q2 Earnings - Critical Data Readout Due This Month

Announcing Q2 earnings, uniQure revealed a cash position of $377m, which management expects to last until the second half of 2027. Net loss for the quarter amounted to $(37.7m), or $(0.69) per share, down from $(1.16) in the prior year quarter.

uniQure stock took a tumble in early April, a fall I would ascribe to the Trump administration's "Liberation Day" tariffs, which could have a negative effect on its business given it is based outside of the US. President Trump has subsequently threatened to introduce tariffs on pharmaceutical products specifically, and to introduce a "most favoured nation" pricing policy in the US, whereby Americans would pay no more for their drugs than whichever nation charges the least.

These headwinds are worth noting, however they are unlikely to derail the success of AMT-130, firstly in terms of securing approval (while there has been fundamental change at the FDA, its stance on gene therapies has generally been positive), and secondly in the commercial markets, given the present unmet need for new and better therapies.

In Q2, uniQure also says it:

Achieved alignment with the FDA on the AMT-130 statistical analysis plan and CMC (chemistry, manufacturing and control) requirements to support a planned BLA submission in the first quarter of 2026

Perhaps even more importantly, management says it "expects to present AMT-130 topline three-year data in September 2025", which feels like a potentially crucial catalyst for the company - if data are positive, it ought to significantly increase the likelihood of accelerated approval. On its Q2 call with analysts, uniQure CEO Matthew Kapusta said:

Our primary efficacy analysis will compare the 3-year change in cUHDRS in high-dose patients to a propensity score matched external control arm using data from the ENROLL-HD natural history study.

After AMT-130 was awarded breakthrough therapy designation in late April, shares recovered most of the value lost earlier in the month, and reached $16 per share by the time Q2 updates were shared.

In terms of CMC requirements, uniQure's Chief Medical Officer Walid Abi-Saab told analysts:

The agency also endorsed our CMC strategy, stating that it should be possible to validate the AMT-130 manufacturing process by leveraging prior knowledge and experience from HEMGENIX, uniQure's approved commercial gene therapy for hemophilia B.

This approach, supplemented with data from additional full-scale GMP batches and a single successful PPQ run may be sufficient to support process validation for the BLA submission.

Furthermore, from an efficacy perspective the CMO added:

we have submitted the final SAP using a propensity score matched external control derived from the ENROLL-HD data set for the primary analysis. A number of additional analyses, including a propensity score weighted external control will be submitted as sensitivity and supplemental analysis.

This update reflects an alignment with the FDA's stated preference and recommendation for propensity score matched natural history controls. The FDA will receive both the propensity score matched and propensity score weighted analysis as disclosed previously.

I have to admit - as a shareholder - to some nerves around both the CMC and the efficacy data - the former as the CMO suggested that prior knowledge of Hemgenix manufacturing "may" be sufficient, not "will" be sufficient, and the latter because of the use of natural history data as opposed to uniQure's own study data, and the switch from propensity reading to propensity matching, although the CMO has told analysts that:

we found that the propensity score methodology in whichever form is quite robust, leading very similar estimate of decline after 3 years regardless of the methodologies. So to answer your question, I will not expect that the results will be materially different if we use propensity score matching propensity score reading.

The CMO also told analysts that uniQure was:

pleased to announce a fourth cohort in the Phase I/II trial of AMT-130 expected to initiate in the third quarter. This open-label single-arm U.S. study will evaluate safety of the high dose of AMT-130 in at least six patients with lower striatal volumes. Patients who would have previously been excluded based on the criteria of a minimal striatal volume can now be considered potentially expanding access to treatment.

This makes me wonder if uniQure has already concluded it may need more study data to support its biologics license application ("BLA"), although the data to be shared this month does sound like a comprehensive package, so perhaps my fears are unfounded, and the goal is simply to try to expand the target patient population, as stated.

The upward trajectory of the share price across the past few weeks can tentatively be interpreted as a positive, as can the following quote from the CEO (in response to an analyst's question on the latest earnings call):

as we've said in the past numerous times, we have very clear and unambiguous feedback with the FDA. In terms of our situation, it is different meaningfully in so much that we're moving forward with clinical outcomes data and long-term clinical outcomes data as opposed to relying on a surrogate biomarker. So we believe this is a robust approach. We're focused on controlling and executing on what we can control, and we're feeling very optimistic about our path forward.

In terms of the administration of AMT-130, it is somewhat gruesome, involving drilling into the head of the patient, inserting a catheter for infusion of the drug, which takes several hours, and involves the process being repeated six times, three times on each side of the brain, then an overnight stay in hospital. With that said, uniQure's CMO says patients:

tend to recover very quickly because, again, it's a very minimal -- how should I say -- very minimally invasive from the perspective of the size of the probe that are introduced and often patients within a few days, they get back to work. At least that's been our experience in our trial.

When prompted on the earnings call to suggest a bar that upcoming data must reach to support accelerated approval, uniQure's CMO suggested:

When we talk to a number of KOLs, what they tell us is, look, anything that is -- that would reduce the disease progression by 25% to 30% would be great.

We saw the data at 2 years. We feel optimistic that the 3-year data will continue to show a meaningful improvement that will both satisfy the patients, but also the FDA.

Concluding Analysis - Am I upholding My Buy Rating On uniQure Stock?

Short answer, yes I am, but I have to admit I do so with some trepidation. In 2025, we have witnessed the promise of a new and fully approved gene therapy, elevidys, approved to treat Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD") and marketed and sold by Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), with revenue expectations of >$2bn in 2025, unravel, on safety grounds, and the therapy be withdrawn from the market for a period.

This underlines the fact that gene therapies are not yet fully derisked on safety grounds, while efficacy can be hard to establish in a real world setting, even when the mechanism of action ("MoA") has been proven to work preclinically and even in a mid-stage study.

AMT-130 has been subject to a clinical hold in the past, but has passed almost every test since, however there is still one more major test to pass in the form of the three year data.

uniQure is not a single product company - Hemgenix manufacturing and royalty revenues came to ~$5.3m in Q2, so is unlikely to be a major value driver for the company long-term, but management spoke positively on the earnings call about the potential of AMT-260 in mesial temporal lobe epilepsy - one patient treated demonstrated 92% seizure reduction with no serious adverse events through first five months of follow up. There are also therapies in development for ALS, Fabry disease, and Alzheimer's.

As such, while investors should expect major share price losses if the 3-year data to be shared this month do not show a statistically significant slowing of disease progression, there would still be a few reasons to be positive about the future of the company.

On a more optimistic note, positive three-year data - which based on research and analysis still seems to me like - narrowly - the likeliest outcome - ought to send shares soaring, as patient's with Huntington's are desperately in need of better therapeutic options, and a disease modifying therapy would be a cut above anything else on the market, potentially, making it likely that uniQure's candidate will achieve blockbuster sales, and earn the company a multi-billion dollar market cap valuation in time.

That is the bull thesis, and while I am nervous ahead of such a massive data read, I continue to anticipate positive news.

data.title: GPIQ: Strong ~10% Distribution Yield Providing Steady Passive Cash Flow
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:GPIQ) is part of a popular group of call writing funds that have made their way to market over the last several years. Call writing funds are not new, but they've been seeing somewhat of a surge as more and more fund sponsors provide funds in this category. Each of them comes with its own varied tweaks in strategy and processes, but each pays a relatively higher "yield."

An important distinction is that the bulk of the payouts are not income yield or a dividend, but the gains that can be realized from option premiums. Therefore, if we want to be technical, it pays "distributions," as that distinguishes the fact that it includes sources other than income alone.

GPIQ Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 10.03% (0.42% SEC yield)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.35%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.332 billion
  • Structure: Active ETF

GPIQ's investment objective is to "seek current income while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation."

To achieve that, the fund "invests, under normal circumstances, 80% of its net assets plus any borrowings for investment purposes in investments in companies that are included in the Fund's benchmark." They continue with "...and selling call options with exposure to the benchmark."

In the case of GPIQ and many of the other call writing funds these days, they write index options rather than on individual holdings within the fund. Given the reference index of the Nasdaq 100, we see a portfolio that isn't unexpected-meaning it mirrors the Nasdaq 100 holdings quite closely.

That includes a substantial amount of concentration in the top 10. Those names account for over 50% of the entire portfolio of ~100 holdings. That also translates into a heavy tilt toward the information technology sector, which comprises 53.3% of the fund's portfolio.

GPIQ Peer Comparison

We can compare GPIQ to several different competitors in the Nasdaq-focused call writing overlay space. The below chart is since Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF's (QDTE) inception on March 7, 2024. GPIQ is a bit older, as it launched on Oct. 24, 2023. QDTE, along with JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), all have slightly different strategies.

That said, all except for JEPQ have shown relatively similar total returns during this period. GPIQ has slightly underperformed QQQI and slightly outperformed QDTE, meaning it's delivered quite respectable results, in my opinion.

Of course, one may be interested to see the comparison of GPIQ against the Invesco QQQ (QQQ). After all, that is the fund's reference benchmark, and we know that call writing funds can limit upside during sharply rising markets-which we've mostly had since GPIQ's inception. In that case, we see exactly what we would expect.

QQQ outperformed during this time, though, when QQQ was falling sharply in April, GPIQ didn't fall as hard. Again, another key feature of call writing funds, during sideways or falling markets, they can outperform because they collect a premium, whereas the straight index does not.

One of the differentiating features of the newer call writing funds is their dynamic approach. That is, they actively overwrite to varying degrees, given what management believes is best. Here is from GPIQ's prospectus specifically:

To generate income, the Fund employs a dynamic options "overwrite" strategy whereby the Fund sells (writes) call options on a varying percentage of the market value of the equity investments in the Fund's portfolio (the "Option Strategy"). The Fund expects that, under normal circumstances, the overwrite level (i.e., the ratio of the notional value of call options sold by the Fund to the market value of the equity investments in the Fund's portfolio) will be between 25% and 75% of the value of the equity investments in the Fund's portfolio. The Fund generally expects to sell call options on underliers that provide economic exposure to the Fund's benchmark, such as an ETF that tracks an index, and not on securities of issuers included in such index and held by the Fund. The Fund is an actively managed ETF, which is a fund that trades like other publicly-traded securities. The Fund is not an index fund and does not seek to replicate the performance of a specified index.

This is a similar process for QQQI and QDTE, with the dynamic approach. QQQI and GPIQ will also "ladder" in their short call positions. As they write options and continuously roll them, they end up selecting different strike prices. For GPIQ, they seem to do a weekly option writing strategy and adjust as needed.

In this way, we can see that GPIQ can perform much better as it can capture more of the upside relative to something like the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD). QYLD simply overwrites nearly 100% of its notional value and constantly does that, which limits upside participation. That group also offers the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call & Growth ETF (QYLG). That fund overwrites around 50% of its portfolio.

When we look back at a comparison between GPIQ, QYLD, QYLG, and QQQ, we see exactly what we would expect to see in terms of results.

In terms of AUM, between these call-writing funds, JEPQ is by far the largest. It has a bit longer history compared to the others, and the strategy was working much better than the other available fund at the time, QYLD. QYLD has stalled in terms of AUM for what appears to be several years. Of the newer funds, GPIQ has seen its AUM rise respectably, but QQQI has really garnered a lot of attention.

GPIQ's Distribution

The distribution yield isn't as high for GPIQ as some of its peers. As of writing, GPIQ is roughly 50% overwritten. QYLD is at ~100%, and QQQI is around 70%. However, at around 10%, that is still a respectable distribution yield that is paid monthly. It's also fairly consistent from month to month, without what I'd consider any meaningful swings.

Many ETFs are generally variable each month, so seeing some close consistency can be welcomed for income investors.

As is generally the case with these call writing funds, they have large characterizations of return of capital distributions. We saw that being the case for both reported periods reflected below.

That can be tax-friendly to investors, as it reduces an investor's cost basis rather than becoming taxable in the year received. Therefore, the distribution may be viewed as tax-deferred to the point of when an investor sells and if a gain is realized at that point. If an investor's cost basis is reduced to zero, which could take many years but isn't impossible, then further ROC distributions are taxed at long-term capital gains.

Conclusion

GPIQ is another offering in the call writing ETF space that brings a dynamic approach to its overwrite strategy. That includes the degree of how far out-of-the-money they are writing the calls, and the percentage overwrite can be tweaked as well, given different environments. Having this greater flexibility can be a positive when it comes to participating in more upside potential relative to other call writing funds, which take a more mechanical and passive approach. Still, in sharply rising Nasdaq 100 periods, GPIQ is subject to some upside cap on some of its portfolio to a degree. The trade-off for that is the monthly distribution that the fund can pay out to its investors.

data.title: The Williams Companies: Positioned For Growth From Natural Gas Demand
data.description:
The The Williams Companies, Inc. (NYSE:WMB) is one of the largest midstream energy companies in the United States. The company owns natural gas pipelines stretching across the United States, along with natural gas processing plants, fractionation facilities, storage facilities, and other supporting infrastructure:

The legend for the map above is here:

We can see that the company has a presence in most of the major basins in which natural gas is produced, including the Permian Basin, the Marcellus Shale, and the Haynesville Shale. However, the company only has limited exposure to the Bakken Shale in North Dakota. This may be due to the fact that, historically, the Bakken Shale was primarily an oil-producing basin. Until very recently, its production of natural gas was quite limited:

We can see that prior to 2018 or so, the Bakken shale was only producing less than two billion cubic feet of natural gas per day. Even the most recent figures state that the natural production of that region is far less than that of the Permian Basin or Appalachia. Unlike many other large midstream companies, The Williams Companies focuses almost exclusively on natural gas. It has no long-haul oil transportation pipelines at all, and its only oil gathering pipelines are located offshore in the Gulf. Thus, it makes some sense for the company to have only very limited exposure to the Bakken Shale since that region was not a significant source of natural gas until very recently.

With that said, of course, The Williams Companies’ primary focus on natural gas may have given it a decided advantage over some of its more diversified peers over the past few years. Over the past five years, The Williams Companies has delivered a 263.39% total return, dwarfing the performance of either Kinder Morgan (KMI) or Enbridge (ENB), two of the company’s largest peers:

One of the biggest reasons for this is that the fundamentals for natural gas are much stronger than the fundamentals for crude oil. In short, the demand for natural gas in the United States is expected to grow at a much more rapid pace than the demand for crude oil. While both Kinder Morgan and Enbridge both have some exposure to natural gas, The Williams Companies is much more of a pure-play on natural gas demand than either of those other two companies so it makes sense that it would derive greater benefits from the strong fundamentals.

Unfortunately for income-oriented investors, which is a category that applies to many individuals who are considering making an investment in midstream energy, the strong recent performance of the company’s stock price has resulted in The Williams Companies having the lowest yield of the three midstream energy giants:

Company Name

Current Yield

The Williams Companies

3.47%

Kinder Morgan

4.38%

Enbridge

5.68%

As we can clearly see, The Williams Companies has the lowest yield of the three midstream energy giants. This could be something of a turn-off for potential new investors since it means that an investment in it would generate a lower level of income than would an investment in either of the other two firms. Many investors who are in search of income would prefer to maximize the income that they get from their portfolios, and a higher-yielding asset tends to be better suited to this purpose than one with a lower yield. However, we should keep in mind that The Williams Companies has a fairly long history of growing its dividend over time. Over the past five years, The Williams Companies has grown its dividend at a 4.56% average annual rate, which is significantly higher than the other two:

Company Name

5-Yr. Dividend Growth Rate

The Williams Companies

4.56%

Kinder Morgan

2.51%

Enbridge

2.55%

This alone is something that could be quite important in the current macroeconomic environment. As I pointed out in a previous article, Enbridge’s dividend growth has not kept up with inflation, and as such, its dividend growth has been negative in real terms. The same is true for Kinder Morgan. It is something of a different story for The Williams Companies. The company paid a dividend of $0.40 per share in September 2020, and its most recently declared dividend was $0.50 per share, which will be paid at the end of this month. The company has therefore increased its dividend by $0.10 per share over the past five years.

If we assume that the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers accurately reflects the rate of inflation, then a $0.40 per share dividend in 2020 would have the same buying power as a $0.50 per share dividend today. Thus, unlike its two peers, The Williams Companies has actually managed to keep up with inflation. This is something that is very important for anyone who is actually trying to use their portfolio’s income to support themselves, as bills and other lifestyle expenses have risen over the same period, so investors need the income provided by their portfolios to rise at a similar rate in order to simply maintain their lifestyles. The Williams Companies was the only one of the three companies that managed to deliver on that need over the past five years.

In addition to this, the company’s dividend growth has allowed investors who got in early to end up earning a very high yield on their invested capital. I first initiated coverage of The Williams Companies to Energy Profits in Dividends subscribers on January 7, 2020. The stock’s closing price on that date was $22.19 per share. Anyone who purchased the stock on that date is currently earning a 9.01% yield on the money that they initially invested. That is certainly a much more attractive yield than today’s price, and it continues to increase year after year. Should The Williams Companies continue to grow its dividend over time, then anyone purchasing the stock, even at today’s price, will have a more attractive yield-on-cost after a few years.
