As we will see over the remainder of this article, it is reasonable to expect this outcome.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed The Williams Companies at Energy Profits in Dividends was back on June 1, 2025. At the time, I pointed out that the continual and rapid construction of new data centers to support generative artificial intelligence deployments could be a driver of growth for the company. After all, between artificial intelligence data centers and increasing adoption of electric vehicles, the demand for electricity is expected to increase by 32% by 2040. It is not feasible to expect that renewable energy will be able to scale sufficiently to provide all of the needed incremental energy, given its intermittent and unreliable nature. Nuclear power and natural gas seem most likely to be the two sources of energy that will see growing use as electric providers attempt to make sure that the grid remains stable and is capable of maintaining the “always-on” characteristic that most of us expect.

In addition to this, data center operators have begun to install natural gas turbines at the data center itself in order to provide the electricity that is needed to power the equipment located on site. This is at least partially due to electric utilities being unable to provide all of the electricity that the data center demands in a timely manner.

The Williams Companies benefits from growing natural gas usage because its industry presence is significant enough that its pipeline volumes will likely increase due to the increased amount of natural gas that needs to be transported to meet consumption needs. After all, The Williams Companies is, at its core, a midstream company whose cash flows directly correlate with the amount of resources that move through its pipelines.

The Williams Companies released its second-quarter 2025 earnings report since the last time that we discussed it. This earnings report was on the whole quite solid, as the company reported a massive 8.46% year-over-year increase in its adjusted EBITDA:

This is generally supportive of the growing demand for natural gas thesis that I outlined in previous articles on The Williams Companies. The company outright stated in its earnings press release that:

“Williams delivered another outstanding quarter with Adjusted EBITDA up 8% over second quarter last year, driven primarily by Transco expansions and new volumes in the Gulf as well as higher volumes in our Northeast and West gathering and processing segments.”

Higher volumes historically correlate directly with higher adjusted EBITDA for this company:

Thus, the growing amount of natural gas being consumed in the United States is driving up the company’s volumes and cash flows. This works perfectly with the longstanding thesis that we have had for this company.

As we can see, there could be some reasons to expect the company’s stock price to have increased since our last discussion on The Williams Companies. After all, the company put out strong earnings and still has significant forward earnings growth prospects. When combined with the overall strength in the domestic equity markets since early June, we can expect some share price gains.

However, this was not the case, as we can see here:

Despite the very strong earnings report and the cash flow growth, the share price of The Williams Companies is down since our last discussion on it. This might be due to the fact that the price of natural gas is down 16.83% over the same period:

One thing that regular readers may recall from previous articles is that the share prices of midstream companies frequently correlate with energy prices despite the fact that the cash flows of these companies do not. In the case of The Williams Companies, the volume of resources matters much more than the actual price of natural gas. Nevertheless, the decline in natural gas prices since early June could be a reason why the stock price is down despite the strong earnings report.

About The Williams Companies And Growth Drivers

As mentioned in the introduction, The Williams Companies is one of the largest midstream companies in the United States, and it is the largest that is nearly exclusively focused on natural gas. This natural gas focus has allowed it to be one of the better-performing midstream companies since the COVID-19 pandemic caused energy companies to crash.

The fact that The Williams Companies is almost exclusively focused on natural gas positions it well to profit from the growing demand for natural gas. As of August 2025, there are 58 coal-fired electric generating plants operating in the vicinity of the company’s existing pipeline infrastructure:

These coal-fired power plants have a nameplate generation capacity of 55 gigawatts. If all of these coal-fired power plants were to shut down and convert to natural gas-fired power plants, it would increase the consumption of natural gas in the company’s infrastructure footprint by 8.4 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day. To put that in perspective, The Williams Companies transported an average of 14.6 million dekatherms of natural gas per day through its infrastructure in the second quarter of 2025. That is approximately 14.6 billion cubic feet, so the replacement of all of the coal-fired electric generation within the footprint of the company’s existing infrastructure would require 57.5% of the company’s current natural gas transmission volume.

Granted, The Williams Companies will not wind up with an increase of 57.5% of its volumes just from the retirement of coal-fired generation within its footprint. While it is true that regulators in many states have been pushing electric utilities to retire their coal-fired power plants, the retired capacity will not be entirely replaced with new natural gas-fired generation capacity. Furthermore, there is no guarantee that The Williams Companies will receive all of the contracts that may be issued to transport this natural gas to any replacement natural gas plants that are constructed within these regions of the country. However, this example does serve to show how much growth is possible simply from this growth driver alone, and it is likely that The Williams Companies will end up receiving some benefits from it.

There are some other notable drivers of natural gas demand growth that should benefit The Williams Companies going forward. One of the biggest of these is liquefied natural gas, which is expected to become one of the more important products in the global energy space over the remainder of this decade. According to the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis, various participants in this energy space will construct new liquefied natural gas plants that will produce a total of 180 million tonnes per annum of liquefied natural gas over the 2025 to 2028 period:

The bulk of this capacity will be constructed in the United States, Qatar, Russia, Canada, and Africa, with the United States being the largest. There are currently five liquefied natural gas plants currently under construction, which by themselves have a nameplate capacity of 71 million tonnes per annum in aggregate. These five projects are:

  • Plaquemines LNG (18 mtpa)
  • Golden Pass LNG (16 mtpa)
  • Rio Grande LNG (15 mtpa)
  • Port Arthur LNG (12 mtpa)
  • Corpus Christi LNG expansion project (10 mtpa).

A few of these projects are located within the vicinity of The Williams Companies’ massive Transco pipeline system, which is the large one shown in the map provided above that extends from Texas through New Jersey and New York. As of today, there are liquefaction plants under construction that would require an aggregate of 10.5 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day to produce to their nameplate capacity. That by itself is 71.92% of The Williams Companies’ total natural gas transmission pipeline volume in the second quarter of 2025.

In addition to the projects that are currently under construction, industry participants have proposed and evaluated the feasibility of constructing even more liquefaction plants that would be located within the footprint of The Williams Companies’ infrastructure. If these proposed projects actually end up being built, they would require another 13.3 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day to operate at capacity. Thus, clearly, the amount of natural gas that could potentially be required by the liquefied natural gas export industry exceeds the total transportation capacity of The Williams Companies’ infrastructure, and remember that this is in addition to natural gas consumption that could arise from the retirement of coal-fired natural gas power plants. The potential for growth from these dynamics for a natural gas transportation company like The Williams Companies should be immediately apparent from these projections.

One of the characteristics of pipeline infrastructure is that natural gas pipelines only have a finite capacity of resources that they can handle. Thus, in order to take advantage of this impending demand growth, it is necessary for The Williams Companies to construct additional infrastructure in order to increase the volume of resources that it can transport. The company is doing this, as it has sixteen projects that are currently under construction and scheduled to come online by the end of 2030:

We can see that quite a few of the company’s transmission pipeline projects are scheduled to come online within the next twelve months. These near-term projects include the Commonwealth Energy Connector, Alabama Georgia Connector, Overthrust Westbound Expansion, Stanfield South, Naughton Coal-to-Gas Conversion, and Gillis West. A few of these are simply additions to the Transco pipeline system that will increase the amount of natural gas that can be transported to certain areas. This is mostly a direct response to the needs of the company’s customers, and in fact, The Williams Companies has already secured contracts from its customers for each of the projects shown above. The fact that the company has already secured contracts means that we can be quite certain that each of them will prove to be additive to the company’s cash flow as soon as they become operational.

In addition, reasonable projections can be made about the profitability of each of these projects. Overall, The Williams Companies should be positioned to grow its adjusted EBITDA at a 5% to 7% rate through 2030. That is not as strong as the year-over-year growth that the company delivered in the second quarter of 2025, but it is still a very respectable growth rate for a midstream company.

Financial Considerations

The Williams Companies has long had one of the strongest financial positions of any of the giant midstream corporations. We can see this by looking at the company’s debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio, which essentially tells us how easily the company can carry its current debt load with its pre-tax cash flow. For the twelve-month period that ended on June 30, 2025, The Williams Companies reported an adjusted EBITDA of $7.276 billion. The company had a total debt of $28.5720 billion as of June 30, 2025. This gives the company a total debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 3.93x at the end of the second quarter of 2025.

This is a better ratio than the one possessed by either Kinder Morgan or Enbridge. In its second quarter 2025 earnings press release, Kinder Morgan stated that it had an adjusted EBITDA of $8.072 billion for the trailing twelve-month period. That gave the company a net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 4.0x, which is higher than The Williams Companies’ total debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio. If we calculate Kinder Morgan’s total debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio, we get 4.06x, which is higher than the figure for The Williams Companies. Enbridge’s ratio is even higher, as the most recent earnings report puts it at 4.7x.

Thus, we can clearly see that The Williams Companies has more support for its debt from its pre-tax cash flow than do either of the other two giant North American midstream corporations. This is something that many risk-averse investors might appreciate, since more debt tends to correlate to higher levels of risk.

Conclusion

In conclusion, The Williams Companies has been one of the better-performing of the major midstream corporations over the past few years. This has unfortunately resulted in its dividend yield being lower than its peers. However, it is one of the few midstream companies whose dividend still has the same purchasing power that it did back in 2020 due to The Williams Companies’ superior dividend growth. This company still has significant growth prospects ahead of it, as natural gas demand is likely to continue to grow for the next several years. This should allow the company to grow its adjusted EBITDA at a 5% to 7% rate through 2030.

data.title: Nvidia's China Revenue Constraints Don't Negate The Bull Case
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA), the stock has traded laterally.

Nvidia stock is likely facing more intense pressure on its China revenues following increased political friction that's likely following the 2025 "Victory Day" parade, where President Xi Jinping was seen directly alongside Russian President Vladimir Putin and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un. Given that the overarching message from the day was antithesis to "Western hegemony," there is a rational conclusion to be made that China's isolated nature on the global stage will likely increase following its outright detraction of the current geopolitical status quo.

Nvidia's Relationship With China

For years, China represented a double-digit share of Nvidia's revenue. In FY25, China and Hong Kong accounted for ~13% of total sales, but by Q2 FY26 that figure had fallen to ~6%, underscoring a rapid contraction. Singapore often masks U.S. demand, with >99% of Singapore-billed controlled compute sales actually headed to U.S. customers in recent quarters, not to China, which means that Nvidia's China revenue statements are clear and direct—we can essentially take them at face value.

At the moment, shipments of high-end AI accelerators to China are license-dependent, and many data center GPUs made by Nvidia fall within those thresholds. In August 2025, some H20 licenses were granted for sale to China, but Nvidia has not shipped or booked these, primarily because China is focusing on developing its own chips. China, by all indications, wants "independence," or isolation.

Recent optics will almost certainly tighten the policy bias. The "Victory Day" parade with Xi flanked by Putin and Kim Jong Un does not change the law, but it will almost certainly strengthen the hawkish frame in Washington, raising the odds of narrower, slower, or rescinded licenses.

When U.S. controls bite, Beijing backs domestic substitutes—a strategy it is leaning on more and more recently. The main vectors for this are Huawei Ascend (CANN/ModelArts stack), Biren BR100 (Hot Chips), and Moore Threads MTT S3000/MUSA. It's worth looking a little deeper into these companies for reflection on Nvidia's relative position to China's rising infrastructure.

Huawei was founded by Ren Zhengfei, who established the company in 1987 and remains its CEO. It's a privately held company, and its Ascend AI chips compete with Nvidia's GPUs, while its CANN + ModelArts is a software ecosystem designed to replace Nvidia's CUDA. Huawei is essentially the closest domestic stand-in for Nvidia in China.

Biren Technology has a goal to be China's high-end alternative to Nvidia in data centers. It was founded by Zhang Wen (ex-SenseTime) with veterans from Qualcomm (QCOM), Nvidia, and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The company has built the BR100 GPU (an Nvidia H100-class competitor) on TSMC (TSM) 7nm with 77B transistors. The production is inevitably hit by U.S. export rules, so the ecosystem is still limited, but the technology is catching up.

Moore Threads is another rising Chinese semiconductor behemoth, founded in 2020 by Zhang Jianzhong, an ex-Nvidia VP. The team is stacked with ex-Nvidia engineers, including CTO Zhang Yubo. Its MTT S3000 and MUSA software stack is designed as an Nvidia CUDA ecosystem clone. This is perhaps one of the most stark examples of Chinese mimicry, and a potential direct long-term threat to America's geopolitical leadership. China appears to be directly vying for full industrial independence.

Given this environment, and broader, more complicated geopolitical competition between China and the West, we can see that regulatory pressures on China from the West will likely persist. To counter the U.S. export controls limiting Nvidia's ability to ship advanced GPUs to China, China's state funding is flowing directly to Huawei, Biren, and Moore Threads to reduce reliance on Nvidia. For Nvidia, China offers capped and lower-quality revenue that's license-dependent, and the West will inevitably remain the company's core growth engine. That said, just because China is focusing on its isolation, which it frames as "independence," that doesn't mean the rest of the world has to, or will, follow suit. Perhaps America needs greater independence from China, but America does not need independence from the rest of the world. This is a very positive elementary difference between the West and China and bodes much stronger for Nvidia than its Chinese counterparts. Nvidia has access to an open international architecture of markets, while China will be more limited to self-serve.

Nvidia's Thriving Western-Oriented Bull Case

CEO Huang himself has envisioned the AI infrastructure boom remaining massive through 2030, with a forecast of $3–4 trillion total addressable market by the end of the decade. Nvidia currently commands over 80% of the GPU market for AI training and deployment, pushing the likelihood of over $1 trillion in Nvidia's annual data-center revenue as likely if it can capture 55–60% of the market as it becomes saturated with more competition and diversification.

The truth is that despite China containment, Nvidia's Western-oriented architecture is the dominant growth engine of the global AI economy. The United States and its allies, including Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOGL, GOOG), Meta (META), Oracle (ORCL), and European hyperscalers, remain the largest, fastest-growing, and most capital-intensive spenders on accelerated compute. Put plainly: the bulk of the $3–4 trillion AI infrastructure supercycle is flowing through Western-aligned capital markets, clouds, and enterprises—this is the very ecosystem that Nvidia dominates and China is constrained from accessing. Put even more bluntly: Chinese Nvidia substitutes have their growth horizon capped predominantly to domestic use, while Nvidia can access the remaining economy of 7 billion people readily (this number excludes China's 1.4 billion population). This is not cold war, it is simply the reality of polycentrism and the nature of economically/ideologically allied vs. non-allied economies.

Valuation Update

Nvidia stock is currently positioned at a good entry point. The secular AI thesis is very much alive, and any fear about multiple compression is more than offset by structural medium-term growth remaining intact. In other words, moderation in returns does not mean contraction in returns, and likely future alpha is still present.

The stock's 14-day RSI is now 40, indicating moderately good value, especially when positioned alongside the price trading along the 50-day moving average.

We may be looking at Nvidia's forward P/E as likely trending down toward 25 over the next five years (the ratio is currently about 38). Even from the following chart, the stock should probably be trading around its average forward P/E right now, given the growth moderation we're facing. So, maybe NVDA is a little overvalued, but that doesn't really bother me (the growth is too good).

So, what are we dealing with in terms of a return estimate? If normalized EPS reaches $9.50 in January 2030, approximately four years away, and the forward P/E is 25 in January 2029, we're looking at a stock price of about $240 in three years. The current price is $170, so that's only about a 12% three-year CAGR. Not bad, but not great.

But let's backtrack a bit. What about in two years? If January 2029 normalized EPS is $8.50, and January 2028 forward P/E is 30, the stock price will be $260, or a two-year CAGR of 23%. I think setting a take-profit for Nvidia of $250 is wise. Beyond that point, the annual return diminishes, and contraction/volatility risk rears its head. Nvidia is a high-growth ballast right now, not a world-class 30–40% compounder anymore. It's still worth owning, but not forever. 1.5 years from today and exiting cleanly with respect for the company's ongoing growth with ~20% stock returns in a bull case is the best an elite allocator can do. If you prefer the long course, just recognize the diminishing returns on the horizon.

Risk Assessment

There are two main risks with Nvidia stock I've already outlined in this thesis:

  1. Hawkish U.S. policy as China is doubling down on undemocratic allegiances.
  2. Valuation multiple compression as the AI infrastructure growth trajectory naturally matures.

However, there are also other risks that deserve some attention here. We are all very aware of the macro and thematic bull case for Nvidia, but the risks are more subtle and nuanced (beyond the valuation, growth moderation, and China revenue constraints).

As of Q2 2026, Nvidia's largest customer comprised 23% of its total revenue, with its second largest customer at 16%. This comes at a time when hyperscalers are also developing in-house chips to reduce dependency on Nvidia. Even more broadly, the decision to significantly reduce AI capex by a major customer, like Meta Platforms (META), could cause Nvidia's valuation to bleed substantially overnight.

There's also the looming power constraints that could throttle customer deployments. If a campus can't secure megawatts and cooling, racks will be sitting idle, and orders would most likely slip for Nvidia.

These are the main risks investors have to content with, and in the next 1.5 years, I see none of them being severe enough to reduce the world-class return potential. I'm staying bullish on and invested in Nvidia, for now.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Nvidia was 15% of my portfolio for a while, but it's now at about 8%. I feel comfortable holding on a while longer, but I do recognize that there are limitations here to long-term recurring alpha. Maybe it will be just 1.5 years before alpha diminishes, or maybe it will be 5—the trick is not to take your eye off the ball. With a company like Nvidia, the investment should be analyzed thematically and with macro awareness, as it will be the AI infrastructure capex cycle that determines Nvidia's AI-led valuation longevity. Of course, there are new verticals like robotics Nvidia can shift its dominant focus into, but I wouldn't bank on that being as aggressive a compounding trajectory without any meaningful cyclical downturn in the interim. Still, for now, Nvidia is a Strong Buy, and I'm holding on for the ride.
